content.problemStatement:
You have a binary string SSS of length NNN.

You must perform the following operation on the binary string SSS **exactly once**:


Choose two integers LLL and RRR (1≤L≤R≤N)(1 \le L \le R \le N)(1≤L≤R≤N) and invert the substring SL…RS_{L \dots R}SL…R​ (i.e change 111 to 000 and change 000 to 111).
Determine whether you can make the number of zeroes in SSS equal to number of ones in SSS by performing the above operation exactly once. If there exists a way, also output the bounds of the chosen substring.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of string SSS.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS of length NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output `NO` if there is no way to make the number of zeroes equal to number of ones.


Otherwise, output `YES`. In the next line, output two integers LLL and RRR (1≤L≤R≤N)(1 \le L \le R \le N)(1≤L≤R≤N) — bounds of the chosen substring. If there are multiple answers, print any.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
constraints: SSS is a binary string, i.e, contains only characters 000 and 111
Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: We can pick L=1L = 1L=1 and R=2R = 2R=2 to obtain: 01‾→10\underline{01} \rightarrow 1001​→10, which contains equal number of zeroes and ones.
Test case 2: It can be proven that there is no way to make the number of zeroes equal to number of ones.
Test case 3: We can pick L=4L = 4L=4 and R=4R = 4R=4 to obtain: 1000‾→1001100\underline{0} \rightarrow 10011000​→1001, which contains equal number of zeroes and ones.
